speaker: interviewer
text: Zvi, You were telling us when you met up with the Russian partisans--

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually I find them by searching couple nights. I used run from one farm to the other farm to catch him, to meet him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me a week till I find end of-- end of the-- of the [INAUDIBLE], like to say, to get him [INAUDIBLE]. And then after I got him, was-- first one was two of them went together to a farm for food. And then I met him. And I tell him, [INAUDIBLE] kind of way, and I would like to join your group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, they took me with. We went in deeper and  deeper. And that was a big forest. It's a famous forest, they used to call it [INAUDIBLE], of 10-- 10 kilometers in range. And what I find out, when they took me to the group-- they have two houses. I find out some of them were running away from-- from the army. I mean, not to get-- they didn't-- they didn't get into the plant from the Germans. They didn't give in when they run away to the forest. Some of them were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] brought in from Russia as criminals to build [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] army buildings, or aerodromes for airplanes. You know they were sentenced to 20 years, 25 years, not political but criminals. So the attitude of these people were different from the soldiers who got left from the army. But anyway, they organize themself in [? group. ?] They are same kind. It was a count, and I am Jewish. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is May 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you spend with the Russian Partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spend with them a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go back to Juzia. But I find out they are not my companion. I can never survive with them. They put me in guard. At night, they used to go out looking for drink, looking for girls. And they used to put me in guard to guard them. I said, that's not a way. I'm not going to survive with them. They're not caring to survive. They're caring of the day, [? they ?] go drink-- to drink till-- till they're drunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you go back to Juzia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I go back to Juzia. When I tell them what I find out with the Russian partisans, They said, they are not partisans. They are a bunch of crooks. They are looking on daily basis to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now how long did you stay with Juzia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I stay another couple days. When I went in into the Jewish, groups, well, I find out they were in hiding. And somehow I didn't like the way they took what kind of measures they took to survive. I didn't like it. I know already the whole surrounding from Radun to-- in-- in a certain, let's say in a range of 50 miles. I know the way, every step. I used to recognize a barking from a dog to who it belongs. I used to go in in a farm--

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you stay with the Jewish partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't partisans. They weren't in hiding-- there were in hiding. No other partisan's activities, they just to survive. In they time stay in hiding, and I attempt to go out and get some food to eat for the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stay with them till 1944, all kind of crooks. I didn't like the way they're behaving, between them. But that's a story for itself. It's not the nicest story, but you're going to ask. I'm not going to tell. That was so egotistic, everybody was thinking just for himself, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not thinking your way if you're going to murder, survive, if you're going to get killed. Everybody was just worrying about his one, about himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did stay with them until 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Periodically. When I saw--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated by a peasant farmer. His name was Wojewodski. And the same kind, as usual on a-- [? Arboshefsky ?]. His name was Wojewodski. With--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you liberated by him?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Two German-- the German-- the Russians were pushing [INAUDIBLE]. And the German got [INAUDIBLE] till the Russian came in in our regime. And then I came out to Eishyshok. After two days, and it was also a danger, I did it, because they didn't like surviving Jews coming into the city back. I came in into the city two days after liberation. The Russian army went already by of Vilna, 50 kilometers westward. And the first peasant, I came in into the town. I sit down, and I step, surrounding me. Oh, Michaelowski, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]-- my mother's name-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You're still alive. I say, see I am still alive. I have [INAUDIBLE] with me anyway to protect myself. But the way they accept me was with such a [INAUDIBLE] that's still somebody's alive to you. I tell him. I [? take ?] another 15 Jews away from Eishyshok. And they soon will come in. But I feel not safe to go in into the town, in Eishyshok. With nobody else and the survivors comes in, when I had really-- if they comes, I came back to Wojewodski, and I tell him how they accept me. And it's still dangerous. So I wait another two days with Wojewodski. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some Jews who survived in the neighborhood, in the area, they came back to Eishyshok. I came back to Eishyshok. We find our self, about 50 survivors, coming back. They occupy the houses, empty houses, from Jews who got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine house was taking off. They took off the whole house. They needed concretes, I don't know why. They took it off and they build something else from the mine house. Mine house was all destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a-- we live all together, about 15 Jews in two houses, three houses. And eventually after three months, we came in July '44. In October, two months later-- two months later-- believe it or not, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yaffa's mother with a little baby, a year old baby, she got built in the attic, a Pole. The surrounding, the Polacks, the white Polacks. They surrounding the house. I wasn't in the house that time. I was in a different house. When they killed the mother, Yaffa's mother with a little baby who got built in the [? hiding. ?] That's unbelievable what the Polacks, their behavior to the Jews, what their attitude was to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came in, oh, you survived. How did you survive? Because they were shooting us in the forest. The Germans wasn't going in into the bushes, into the hidings. They were after us, when the remaining Jews who survived, from the massacres, from Radun, from [INAUDIBLE], from Eishyshok. Got killed by them. We survived from [INAUDIBLE] massacre about 200, we survived, about 17 or 18 people. The rest got killed not from the Germans, but from the Polacks. How long did you stand in Eishyshok?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in Eishyshok till-- that was 1944. It's an old story if I'm going to tell you. I retaliate, to tell you the truth. And I'm not regretting what I retaliate. I knew all the Poles who killed Jews in hiding. I was after them. I retaliate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they catch me. They arrest me, the Russian, for doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you retaliate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put them in jail. Some of them, I killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shooting them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the guns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got the guns. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Well, they were involved in killing Jews. If I catch them, I didn't bring them to prison. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Together with Russian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers, tell them their crimes what they did against Jews not against the Russian soldiers who fall in in the occupation. Anyway, they arrest me, and I run away from the-- from jail. I run away from jail. Maybe I shouldn't have, it's-- it's nothing to [INAUDIBLE] to the Holocaust. That's nothing [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, I went to Germany. From Germany to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Israel from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: With ship. We went out and bought the book in France. We went to Israel with the ship. You know the story of the ship?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you manage to get on the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the help of the Bricha, of the Jewish agencies, helping to immigrate to Israel Jews, young people, and all the people. I was married to her at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married in Germany. And the British wouldn't let us in-- in Israel. They stop us in Haifa. They put us back on three British ships. They brought us back to Germany. I was there in couple months. Then, eventually, the Jewish agency got us false certificates. And we got in Israel. And I was mobilized in the silent army, serving for [? Yanev. ?] And then I became a family man, had two kids. From Israel was 18 years and the circumstances brought me in United States. Now here I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do you live today?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you live today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live today in New York, Queens.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make your living when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got in-- in delivery, in dealing with bakery stuff-- butter, eggs, cheeses, sugar, selling to the bakeries until I retire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's 50 years after the Holocaust, what message would you like to leave for your children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It's very sad and unusual for parents to say to the kids, to the children and the grandchildren, the message what I am going to say-- that they should know [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that I never survived emotional, the Holocaust. I am a split man remaining, up till now. Because my body is still in the grave, my father over me, the blood of my mother, of my father is still on my back. They are with me. They're accompanying me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in my daily life. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] When I am the father and playing with you as a father, I saw you, this new generation of my kids together with my brothers, and my cousins, and my sisters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] once I-- I gave you all I could as a father-- give you education, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I feed you, I dress you. And as much I can, I could, I was with you, playing with you. And playing was the worst part for me. But suddenly you were turning not [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] or not [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Forgive me for that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You were mixed in with all kids who was perished in the grave with me in Eishyshok. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My wife also, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we make love, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I saw my father making love to my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They are part of me, till end of my life. I think you forgive me for that and telling that what it is. But I'm trying always to be after you, to protect you, to give the rest of my life for you till end of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for telling us your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the shul, the main shul in Eishyshok. It was two shuls on both sides-- the outer shul and the [INAUDIBLE] shul. I got in in this main shul with my brother when we find my father in this shul. After Judy came in in this shul, a couple hours later, my father ask me to get out Judy into-- from the shul to escape. We went in like we need to make our needs with Judy, and this window was opened. And because it was impossible. It was there a toilet in the old room was open. So we just got out easily. Nobody stopped us. And I took out Judy from this window, and we went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so this window is the bottom right-hand?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bottom right-hand window?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah, that is my-- in the middle, that's my sister, Edith. The picture was taking a year before the massacre in 1940. To the right side is [? Genessa. ?] They used to call her [? Geneshka, ?] also a shiksa, a girl, a girlfriend. This was from Olkieniki, not from Eishyshok, from the neighboring shtetl. They came also-- they brought the people from Olkieniki and were massacred in the same graves. That is-- that is [INAUDIBLE], her name was [INAUDIBLE]. And this girl, I cannot recall her name, but she's also from Eishyshok. The girlfriend with my sister Edith. And they all got killed in Eishyshok. That is also a group of friends on Eishyshok residence from [? Baytown, ?] organization-- the Zionist organization, [? Baytown, ?] was my sister Chaia, was a member. This is her boyfriend, [? Moshama ?] [? Mihaelofsky. ?] That is his name. These are two brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two kneeling down?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're kneeling down. This is [? Salman. ?] His name, I cannot recall his second name now. This is the brother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [? Salman-- ?] and that second brother it just slipped out from my mind. That is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Standing on the right is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm standing on the right. This is [? Mayaka ?] [? Buyovsky. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's is a girl. Pesh-- Pesha-- [? Peshka ?]. Then [? Literforus ?], everybody was her name. Her name turned to her [? Peshka, ?] and [? Literforus ?] was a doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the person next to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is also a man from Olkieniki, from the neighboring-- from the neighboring shtetl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '39 until the '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any survivors of this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody survived. They all perished in Eishyshok. That is my-- standing in the back, that is my sister. She's Sarah. And that's our neighbor, [INAUDIBLE], the [INAUDIBLE] doctor, [INAUDIBLE]. They were three sisters, but neither of them remember our name. But that is the [? Yankle ?] and [? Blackhaus ?] doctor. And this is her husband, [? Yankle ?] [? Blackhaus ?] doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also was taken in 1940 winter, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did any of them survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, everybody, they all got perished in Eishyshok in the massacre. OK, that is a group of a class with the rabbi Lazar Kropewnicki. He was the rabbi, the teacher of Tanakh. That is a older group. I-- I said the name, [INAUDIBLE] in [INAUDIBLE]. You know, when they're taking in 1927. This is a older group. I recall all the faces, but I cannot recall right now the names, I know him. When I know even the names what's slipped out--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in this second.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time--

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them before they were all growing up kids. They were growing up men in the '20s, in the late '20s when they got killed in the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the last time you saw the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- we were in the same group going from the market, to the massacre, to the graves. It was in the second line of me. When he was saying the prayer before we fall in into the group. His name was Lazar David Kropewnicki. This is also a group from Eishyshok-- from Eishyshok. We're youths belong to Hashomer Hatzair, a Zionist group that's from the left wing. And what I recall, that is in 1926, the path on the side. The only name I remember now, this is [? Kaleko. ?] He--

speaker: interviewer
text: The gentleman in the middle on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the bottom, he was the head of the group. He immigrate to Israel, and he passed away in the-- in the '70s. And this is daughter of [? Katz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: The girl behind him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind him.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. She perished, and she got killed in Eishyshok from-- by the-- in the Holocaust, in the same thing. And I know who they are, but I cannot recall their names. This is a picture from our [INAUDIBLE] school, from the Hebrew school, taken in 1933. That's from our class with head of our teacher, [? Butwhitnik ?]. That is at the-- lady teacher, [? Gotemberg ?]. And that is Szaul, Szaul, the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the names of any of the children in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the children, yes. I'm behind [? Butwhitnik ?]. That is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Behind the teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behind the teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, where I'm pointing out. That is me behind his hat. That is it [? Lebkaramtz ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Right next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to me. And that's just [? Heifetz ?]. And that is [? Tebelablaka ?], the-- the same young fella who jump for [INAUDIBLE], where we were bitten. He fell and broke his leg.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's four to the left of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's [? Tebelablaka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you right there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's is me, yes. And yes, I got to remind myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're-- you're right above the hat of the teacher on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and do you know how many people from this group survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's is from fire department from Eishyshok, the band. And here it's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in also in the late '30s, '38 or '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jews among this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, is Jews. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of them survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: One, nobody survive. This one got killed in the Jewish group while in hiding by accident from another Jew, fixing-- fixing a rifle. That is [? Shepsha ?] [? Sunazone ?]. That is [? Ment ?] [? Kapolatavsky ?]. He-- he immigrate to-- to Israel in-- before the war, when he just also passed away in the '80s not too long ago. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We've got to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that-- that's my oldest daughter born in Israel. Her name Nechama, after my mother. And that is my youngest daughter. The name is Edna, after the mother of my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?
